text: The surgical team utilizes three robotic arms for the thymectomy, with the fourth arm visibly tucked backwards to accommodate the procedure.
The surgeon uses the intuitive XI system to target the scope at the anatomical area of interest, pressing and holding the targeting button to focus on the target anatomy.
The camera port is positioned in the fifth intercostal space along the anterior axillary line, while the robotic arm one is inserted into approximately the third intercostal space.
The first robotic arm is positioned on the left side, lower than the right internal mammary vein, as part of the setup for the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the cadier instrument into the arm located in the third intercostal space during the right-sided thymectomy.
The surgeon places the third port in the seventh intercostal space, creating a V-shaped configuration with the previously positioned ports in the thoracic cavity.
The surgical setup for the right-sided thymectomy is visible, showing three ports arranged in a V formation. A fourth port is sometimes added.
The surgeon prepares for the thymectomy, opting for a subxiphoid approach to remove the tumor, while utilizing a spatula monopolar instrument for dissection.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to address light adhesions between the right lung and the thymoma, employing a right-sided approach.
The phrenic nerve is clearly visible in the hilum as the surgeon uses CO₂ insufflation at a pressure of six millimeters of mercury to keep the lung out of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins freeing fatty and thymic tissue from the pericardium, with an external view showing the surgical setup and an assistant present but not actively engaged.
The surgeon maneuvers three robotic arms within the chest cavity, focusing on the thymectomy procedure. The patient is not myasthenic, so the removal of fatty tissue is not complete.
The surgeon identifies thymic tissue and begins cutting through it, targeting the thin connections between the thymic fat and the pericardium. The thymoma appears as a nicely rounded Type A mass.
The surgeon carefully dissects unnamed structures, using diathermy to navigate through fatty tissue while maintaining proximity to the pericardium.

The surgeon dissects along the phrenic nerve, using diathermy to carefully separate the thymus from the surrounding structures. The dissection progresses towards the superior vena cava, ensuring the thymus does not obstruct the phrenic nerve.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the superior vena cava, ensuring clear visibility of the vessel to avoid unnecessary dissection through surrounding fat tissue.
The surgeon lifts the tissue to expose the superior vena cava (SVC), carefully tracking the dissection near the phrenic nerve.
The surgeon dissects upwards towards the right internal mammary vein, visible in the distance, aiming to expose the innominate vein as it branches off.
The surgeon dissects upward, revealing the reamer vein, and continues tracking along it. After releasing the pleura, the innominate vein becomes visible, extending medially.

The surgeon identifies the reamer artery lateral to the reamer vein. The pleura is opened to the left of the reamer vein within the triangle formed by the superior vena cava, reamer vein, and reamer artery, allowing visualization of both sides of the reamer vein.

The surgeon gains a visual impression of the innominate vein and proceeds to dissect deeper over the superior vena cava (SVC) until reaching the pericardium. The dissection focuses on the pericardium, avoiding fat tissue.
The surgeon tracks back towards the thymus, approaching its location while considering whether to include the pericardium in the dissection.
The surgeon dissects the pleura from the back of the sternum, providing additional mobility to the surgical field. This step is performed with ease, enhancing access to the thymus.

The surgeon dissects approximately one centimeter medial to the rima, visible above, within a defined surgical plane. The focus is on careful dissection without the need to remove all surrounding tissue, as the patient is not myasthenic.

The surgeon dissects immediately under the sternum, adhering closely to its posterior surface, while ensuring a substantial amount of fat remains attached to the specimen. The dissection extends across to the left chest.
The surgeon dissects the thymus from the aorta, which is located behind the pericardium, providing a view of the opposite phrenic nerve.
The surgeon has completed the dissection of the pleura from the sternum and phrenic nerve, forming a triangular space that allows visualization into the neck area.

The surgeon carefully tracks above the innominate vein, identifying a thymic vein. The view shows the thymic vein from the opposite side, highlighting the need for caution due to the potential presence of multiple thymic veins.
The surgeon examines the surgical field, considering the next steps for dissecting the thymoma. The decision involves whether to proceed into the pericardium.
The surgeon lifts the pericardium to tent it slightly and uses diathermy to carefully dissect into the pericardium, addressing adhesions that extend towards the resection margin.

The surgeon uses a cadier to carefully manipulate tissue, ensuring the heart is not affected by diathermy. A disc of pericardium is created beneath the thymoma, providing a safe entry into the pericardium.
The surgeon lifts the pericardium to create a disc, revealing the aorta beneath. The area appears safe for dissection, with no visible dangers present.
The surgeon lifts the pericardium with a spatula at the front of the heart, ensuring a safe dissection as they prepare to create a disc of pericardium, avoiding proximity to the phrenic nerve.
The surgeon has a clear view of the aorta while creating a disc of pericardium. The disc is visible and appears small upon removal.
The surgeon creates a two to three centimeter disc of pericardium medial to the thymoma, with the opposite lung visible in the background.
The surgeon has entered the opposite pleura, revealing the lung. Fatty tissue is being carefully removed to achieve a close margin near the opposite pleura.
The surgeon tracks medially, pulling the thymoma into the right chest to create a clear resection margin, moving in a superior direction.
The surgeon places a retrieval bag into the chest cavity to remove the thymoma and prepares to perform a line of intercostal nerve blocks.
The assistant places a surgical bag into the chest cavity, and the thymoma is carefully positioned into the bag for removal.
A surgical drain is inserted through one of the ports, with the option to use the sub-siphoid port for drainage if available.
